## [00:00 - 03:02]

So what I want to show you here is how I use the CBO DCO strategy, the Infinity retargeting strategy, and to demonstrate that with one of my own ad accounts that I ran this for for one of my in-person mastermind events. So you get to see the inside of how my retargeting looks for that, and perhaps maybe if you've been through the funnel, why you got served these different variants. So first of all, when I'm creating my retargeting, I'm working off the 5W avatar. So I have an idea of what the potential barriers to buy are. So the barriers become the angles for my ad copy. And then secondly, I've gone through my full funnel system and I've created my, you know, my my warm hot, etc. For this specific campaign, I only targeted my warm audiences, which were fans, people that have engaged, email list, etc. And then people that have clicked but not purchased. So they're the only two layers that are running that were running for this. It's long gone. And within this, the way I've set up my DCO, so based on the Infinity retargeting strategy, is that once it was set up, and actually, one thing I will say is I've used this for about, you know, this probably for about, in fact, every mastermind event that I've run, I've used the same ad angles. So here's the thing about this. So if you're in the event space, if you're in the info space, the digital space, if you're selling particular products, the ad angles, the barriers, the reasons why someone wants to buy, they'll be the same. So I think this is the third or fourth event that I literally copied my retargeting campaigns, my ad sets, my angles over, and just literally edited the word. So prior to Milan, I was in Vancouver. So we copied all of the ads from our Vancouver campaign into the Milan campaign, changed out the wording, changed out the URLs, some of the images as well, made it more relevant for this particular mastermind that we ran, and then ran the ads for it. So to show you the structure of what we had, first of all, the ad angles we put in place were based on either promotions, so an offer ending, or based on the angles of what we'd identified previously on being the barriers of people attending an event like this. So for example, the whole sleep better at night was the kind of anxiety related to running your ad accounts. And what we actually found was when we were retargeting, so bear in mind, this is a fairly high ticket purchase. So tickets went for give or take two or three K to attend the mastermind. So my retargeting was a case of not just breaking down their barriers to buy, but helping them convince themselves as to why they needed to attend.

## [03:02 - 06:02]

So for example, sleep better at night, which was I think one of the two, yeah, I think that was like the best performing angle overall. It was more around, for example, if you attend the event, you follow the process, we're going to speed up your understanding, we're going to help you implement parts of the BPM method and kind of help you get faster progress. It will then reduce your anxiety running ads. It will reduce your stress day to day, and therefore get you better results. And that ad angle has performed well for us for every event that we run in terms of retargeting people to attend the event, because it's based on customer testimonials, talking about that kind of thing. So it's not just me coming up with an ad angle and saying, let's test this out. This is based on me understanding, once people have attended, what kind of transformations they've achieved. So the ad angle here is reiterating. So the funnel is people that are on my email list and in my group. So that's who the general message goes out to. There's no cold targeting going on. So I'm not pushing this to an audience that doesn't know me. My warm campaigns here, this was literally one ad, which was a Facebook live that I did. And in that Facebook live, I talked about the actual event, what was coming up, what to expect, etc, etc. So it's a very basic funnel. So actually, everything went into this ad here. So when we announced it in email, pushed it into this post, which was on my page. And from there, we created, looked at retargeting audiences, getting people into the funnel, etc. is literally this is the event. This is what we're doing, etc. That was my warm. That's all it was. So it's a real simple, basic push. And we didn't need anything more than that. And you can see, initially, we drove some sales through this process. So let's have a look at the actual number of sales. So seven purchases just came through that. And then the remaining nine that came through ads, and bear in mind, some didn't come through ads, some was organic. Nine then came through retargeting. So then retargeting here is people that have visited the site and not checked out. And what I would do is create those angles ahead of time. And then I would test three angles at any given time. And what I mean by test is one of the questions I get is how do we do testing for warm and hot audiences? Do we follow the graduation testing process? I do not. And I do not do that because especially where audience sizes are small, I need to test in my live accounts. But by this point, it's not like with prospecting, where you don't really know your avatar and your testing ads and angles. By this point, I know something about them because they've clicked through my ads at the top of funnel. They've visited my site. ## [12:06 - 15:10]

But there are backups. This one, for example, your business might fail, has previously worked well as well. So just nudging them into the fact that if you don't take action now, because you've shown interest, you're not taking action, what's holding you back, didn't need to use it. So the way I queue this up is in my retargs in CBO, I set the budget. It was literally just five pounds daily because it's a small audience. You didn't need to really push too much. I set my ad angles up within each ad angle. I'm setting up multiple copy variants. So as you saw the sleep at night, I've got three copy variants, two or three images, couple of headlines. And I'm just letting Facebook dynamically test. And the way DCO works is as long as your objective at the ad set level is set, so ours was the purchase objective, then it will find people that are likely to purchase and it worked for us. So we didn't need to micro optimize at this level. Facebook just spent wherever the performance was performing. And we just left it. I didn't I didn't need to do any more optimizations or tracking or monitoring because as long as I'm getting the performance that I need, then I'm kind of okay with that. So that's an example of how I set up the kind of inner workings of that DCO based infinity retargeting strategy because this audience is constantly filling up. So just take it with context and don't don't get stressed out if you're retargeting frequencies are super high. There is a balance to be had because you don't want lots of negative feedback from your ad account as well. Because, you know, if people are really not enjoying your ads and you're getting a lot of negative feedback, which you can see in the training on quality scores, you want to make sure you've got that balance right as well. But ultimately, with the DCO format for this, we set the ads, we let Facebook optimize.

## [18:10 - 19:17]

You can optimize on top of that and take out particular variants are not performing. And also one of the other things to bear in mind is each of these ad sets are targeting exactly the same audience. So this is anyone that's visited the sales page and not purchased all ages, all genders. And that's it. It's a simple, simple retargeting. The retargeting was anyone in the last 30 days that's visited. And that's it. I just let Facebook do the rest of it. So it's not like if you visit in three days, get this ad, seven days, get that ad, 10 days, get that ad. I'm letting Facebook do the heavy lifting on this. And this is also how we start our ecom retargeting just straight up 30 days. Viewed content and added to cart doesn't matter. DCO and let Facebook start delivering and let Facebook start bringing in the performance. Then over time, we'll start analyzing it and going deeper and saying, right, maybe we need now need to split VC. People that view the product page from those that viewed the cart page and start serving different ads. But until then, until our audiences are bigger and mature and we know something about them, just let Facebook do the heavy lifting and it makes such a difference to performance.
